name: Cruz de la Chimba Trail
name_original: Vistas sobre el embalse del Quiebrajano - circular trailrunning por el parque periurbano Monte la Sierra
description: Jog the difficult 6.1-mile Cruz de la Chimba Trail through a coffee plantation, gaining 1393 feet with views of the Santa Ana mountains.
region_intro: Jogging around Jaén offers diverse landscapes, from expansive olive groves to rugged mountains and deep gorges. The region is characterized by its numerous natural parks, including the vast Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas, providing varied terrain for runners. Riverine areas and reservoirs, such as the Río Guadalquivir and Rumblar reservoir, offer tranquil, flatter running options. This varied topography ensures a range of running experiences across the province. Best jogging routes around Jaén The most popular jogging route is Olive Oil Greenway Loop from Jaén, a 9.3 miles (15.0 km) trail that takes 1 hour 42 minutes to complete. This route follows a former railway line through scenic olive groves. Another top favourite among local runners is Cerro del Tambor and La Mella refuge - trail running from Santa Catalina castle, a difficult 5.9 miles (9.6 km) path. This route offers challenging climbs and views from Santa Catalina castle. Local runners also love the Cruz de la Chimba Trail, a 6.1 miles (9.8 km) trail leading through varied terrain with significant elevation gain, often completed in about 1 hour 19 minutes. Jogging around Jaén is defined by expansive olive groves, rugged mountains, and picturesque river valleys. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy riverside paths to challenging trail running in natural parks. The routes in Jaén are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 45 reviews. More than 2500 runners have used komoot to explore Jaén's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in Jaén?
A: Jaén offers a wide variety of running routes, with over 90 options available on komoot. These routes cater to different fitness levels, ranging from easy riverside paths to challenging trail runs through mountainous terrain.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when running in Jaén?
A: Runners in Jaén can expect incredibly diverse terrain. The region is famous for its expansive olive groves, often referred to as the 'sea of olive trees,' providing scenic, often flatter paths. You'll also find rugged mountains, deep gorges, and picturesque valleys within its numerous natural parks, such as the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas, and Sierra Mágina. Riverine areas and reservoirs offer tranquil, flatter running options.

Q: Are there easy running routes suitable for beginners in Jaén?
A: Yes, Jaén has several easy running options perfect for beginners or those looking for a relaxed pace. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e989209909/olive-oil-greenway-loop-from-jaen" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Olive Oil Greenway Loop from Jaén&lt;/a&gt; follows a former railway line, offering a smooth surface through scenic olive groves. There are 8 easy routes in total, providing gentle gradients and enjoyable scenery.

Q: Are there challenging trail running options in Jaén?
A: Absolutely. For experienced trail runners seeking a challenge, Jaén's natural parks provide rugged mountains and significant elevation gains. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e989164987/cerro-del-tambor-and-la-mella-refuge-trail-running-from-santa-catalina-castle" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerro del Tambor and La Mella refuge - trail running from Santa Catalina castle&lt;/a&gt; offer demanding climbs and rewarding views. There are 30 difficult routes available, ensuring plenty of options for high-intensity training.

Q: What do other runners say about the routes in Jaén?
A: The running routes in Jaén are highly regarded by the komoot community, boasting an average rating of 4.6 out of 5 stars from over 45 reviews. More than 2500 runners have explored Jaén's varied terrain using komoot, often praising the stunning natural beauty, well-maintained paths, and the unique experience of running through olive groves and historic landscapes.

Q: Can I find circular running routes in Jaén?
A: Yes, many of Jaén's running paths are designed as circular routes, allowing you to start and finish at the same point without needing to retrace your steps. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e989209909/olive-oil-greenway-loop-from-jaen" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Olive Oil Greenway Loop from Jaén&lt;/a&gt; is a prime example, offering a convenient and scenic circular experience through the countryside.

Q: Are there running routes that feature waterfalls or scenic water bodies?
A: Yes, Jaén's natural parks are home to beautiful water features. Within the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park, you can find routes near the Linarejos Waterfall and the famous Borosa River, known for its crystal-clear emerald green natural pools and waterfalls. The Rumblar reservoir in Baños de la Encina also provides a tranquil setting for a run.

Q: Are there running routes that pass by historical sites or cultural landmarks?
A: Many routes in Jaén beautifully integrate natural beauty with historical significance. You can find trails that lead to sites like Malena Waterfall and Yedra Castle within the Cazorla, Segura and Las Villas Sierras National Park. Otiñar Castle, strategically located on a pass, offers a culturally rich landscape to explore. Even urban runs in Jaén city can take you past landmarks like the Jaén Cathedral and San Ildefonso Church. For more on the region's cultural heritage, you can visit &lt;a href="https://en.wikivoyage.org/wiki/Culturally_significant_landscapes_in_Ja%C3%A9n" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Culturally significant landscapes in Jaén&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any natural monuments or viewpoints to see along the running trails?
A: Yes, Jaén's trails offer access to several natural monuments and stunning viewpoints. You can explore highlights like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4754151" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Entrance to the Sierra Mágina Natural Park&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4436595" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ojo de buey&lt;/a&gt;, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4465120" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuente del palo&lt;/a&gt;. For panoramic views, consider routes that ascend to peaks like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2876708" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerro del Tambor Summit&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2839764" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Pandera&lt;/a&gt;, which often feature fire lookout huts or other vantage points.

Q: Are there running routes suitable for families with children?
A: While specific 'family-friendly' designations can vary, many of the easier and moderate routes in Jaén are suitable for families. Paths along riverine areas like the Mota de la Isla by the Río Guadalquivir near Andújar offer easy, flat, and scenic riverside runs. The Vía Verde del Aceite also provides a relatively flat and safe environment for a family outing.

Q: Are there running routes accessible by public transport or with good parking facilities?
A: Many routes around Jaén, especially those closer to urban centers or popular natural park entrances, offer good accessibility. While specific public transport details for each trailhead may vary, routes starting from Jaén city, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6888014" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Jaén Cathedral – Jaén Provincial Council loop from Jaén&lt;/a&gt;, are easily reachable. For routes within natural parks, designated parking areas are generally available at main access points, though it's always advisable to check local information for specific trailheads.
tips: Viewpoint with wonderful views of the Quiebrajano valley, Otiñar and in the distance Jabalcuz and Jaén. In front of us, the majestic La Pandera is shown, with its 1872 meters of altitude.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to jog the Cruz de la Chimba Trail?
faqs.answer: The main route, which is about 9.8 km, generally takes around 1 hour and 19 minutes to jog. If you opt for the shorter 5 km version, it can take approximately 1 hour and 45 minutes at a hiking pace, so jogging would be quicker.

faqs.question: Is the Cruz de la Chimba Trail suitable for beginner joggers?
faqs.answer: While the trail is rated as 'difficult' by komoot due to its elevation changes, it's also described as moderately difficult overall. It offers good cardio and is suitable for individuals in decent shape. Shorter 3 km and 5 km options are available via shortcuts, making it more accessible for those less accustomed to physical activity.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the trail?
faqs.answer: The trail features dirt paths and uneven surfaces, with sections that include uphill and downhill inclines. While some parts can be steeper, they are generally not considered 'steep' overall. During Costa Rica's rainy season (May to November), the trail can become muddy, so good sports or hiking shoes are advisable.

faqs.question: What unique landmarks and viewpoints can I expect to see?
faqs.answer: The trail offers lovely scenic views of the Santa Ana mountains and the Central Valley, including parts of San José. Along the way, you'll find several distinctive landmarks perfect for photos, such as a giant Buddha statue, a 'Costa Rica' sign, a blue morpho butterfly sculpture, a heart sculpture, and the iconic 'La Mano del Mantra' (The Mantra Hand) sculpture. You'll also get a View of Quiebrajano Reservoir.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates sections of several official trails. You'll find yourself on parts of the Cruz de la Chimba, Sendero Cruz de la Chimba, Sendero del Banco, and Sendero a la Fuente de las Pilas.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to jog this trail?
faqs.answer: The best time to jog is outside of Costa Rica's rainy season, which runs from May to November. During the dry season, the paths are less likely to be muddy, offering a more enjoyable experience. However, the lush coffee plants provide a picturesque setting year-round.

faqs.question: Is the Cruz de la Chimba Trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, the trail is pet-friendly! Dogs are welcome, but please ensure they are kept on a leash throughout your visit.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for the trail or the park?
faqs.answer: The Cruz de la Chimba Trail is located within Hacienda La Chimba, which is a private facility. There is typically an entrance fee to access the Hacienda and its trails. It's advisable to check their official website or contact them directly for current pricing and any specific regulations.

faqs.question: Where can I park, and how do I access the trailhead?
faqs.answer: The trail is located at Hacienda La Chimba near San José, Costa Rica. There is parking available on-site at the Hacienda. The trailhead is well-signed from the Hacienda's facilities, making it easy to find and navigate.

faqs.question: What amenities are available at Hacienda La Chimba?
faqs.answer: Beyond the jogging trail, Hacienda La Chimba offers a range of amenities. You can find a restaurant for refreshments after your run, and they also provide other activities such as zip-lining and coffee tours. It's a great place to relax and explore further after your jog.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a jog on this trail?
faqs.answer: Given the uneven terrain and potential for mud during the rainy season, good sports or hiking shoes are highly recommended. Bring water to stay hydrated, especially on warmer days. Sunscreen, a hat, and insect repellent are also good ideas. Don't forget your camera for the many scenic photo opportunities!
